violations.rule: Back brevity / atomicity — no 'and' joining two distinct facts
violations.detail: Back contains two distinct facts joined by 'and': 'It's much faster per update AND can escape shallow local minima due to noise.' These are independent reasons and should be split into two cards.

violations.rule: Back brevity — aim for under 10 words, no dash-elaboration
violations.detail: Back contains a dash-elaboration adding a second fact: 'A point where the gradient is zero but it's neither a minimum nor maximum — the algorithm can stall there.' The definition and the consequence are two separate facts. Split into: what a saddle point is, and why it's problematic for gradient descent.

violations.rule: Atomicity — front asks exactly one thing
violations.detail: Front asks two questions: 'What is a saddle point, AND why is it problematic?' This violates the rule that the front must ask one thing. Split into two cards.

violations.rule: Back brevity — aim for under 10 words
violations.detail: Back contains two definitions joined by a semicolon: 'A local minimum is the lowest point in a neighborhood; a global minimum is the lowest point overall.' This is two facts in one answer. Split into separate cards.

violations.rule: Atomicity — front asks exactly one thing
violations.detail: Front asks about the difference between two things, which requires defining both. This produces a compound back. Split into individual cards testing each concept, or test the distinction more precisely.

violations.rule: Mirror-deducible cards — if knowing card A trivially gives you card B, drop one or merge
violations.detail: Cards 8, 9, and 10 are structurally identical ('X computes the gradient using which portion?') with answers that form a near-exhaustive enumeration of the three variants (all, one, subset). Knowing any two trivially gives you the third. Merge into a multi-cloze or reframe to test non-obvious aspects (e.g., tradeoffs of each).

violations.rule: Back brevity — aim for under 10 words
violations.detail: Back is 17 words: 'Because the gradient points uphill, and the goal is to go downhill toward the minimum.' Could be trimmed to 'The gradient points uphill; subtraction moves downhill.'

Only 1 card (card 13) is counterintuitive/misconception (~7%), and only 1 card (card 14) is application (~7%). The set is heavily skewed toward definitions and concept cards. The guide targets ~20% application and ~10% counterintuitive. Could add cards like: 'What happens to gradient descent on a plateau?' or 'Can gradient descent find the minimum of |x|? Why not?'

violations.rule: Cloze difficulty check — deleted element must not be guessable from context
violations.detail: 'Gradient descent is guaranteed to find the global minimum only when the cost function is {{c1::convex}}' — 'convex' is the single most common answer anyone familiar with optimization would guess here from context. The surrounding phrase 'guaranteed to find the global minimum only when' is a strong pattern-match to 'convex.' Consider reframing to test a less guessable aspect.
